sentences.text: [SWEDISH]
Because my German is non-existent.
Yeah, but I didn't say that in German.
I said that in Swedish.
Yes?
Alfred Winter.
W-I-N-T-E-R.
A-L-F-R-E-D.
The same as-- as well.
No.
Korschenbroich, K-O-R-S-C-H-E-N-B-R-O-I-C-H, in a government district of Dusseldorf, Germany.
Monchengladbach.
No, I grow in the town where I was born, grow up, but I went to school in the city, to high school later on.
We were six children, one girl and five boys.
My sister was Greta.
Then was Kurt, Bruno, Eric, myself, and Herbert.
Well, I'm a twin, and so was Eric.
Julius Winter, and my mother's was Henrietta.
Henrietta.
Her maiden name was Schwarz, S-C-H-W-A-R-Z.
It was a 10-room house.
One live-in maid.
When I was six years old, I went to grammar school for five days a week because since Jewish children would not attend the sixth day, on the Sabbath, we had the day off, we would go to synagogue.
Of course, the town where I was living was Catholic, and there were five Jewish families, but Jewish children would not attend school which is in Germany six days a week.
Later on when I went to high school, I had to go six days a week to school.
Yes, we had a large backyard.
We had quite a bit of land.
My father was a cattle dealer so I grew up just like being on a farm.
Hardly, because we played soccer and handball, something we don't play over here in the United States, and some other games.
And then we played cards and things like that.
My father was also the cantor in the town.
It was a strictly kosher house, but otherwise they were conservative.
My father did not do any business on the Sabbath.
No.
The rabbi only came when there was a wedding or a funeral or something special.
He came from [GERMAN].
His name was Dr.
Gelles, G-E-L-L-ES.
Yes.
Before 1933, there was no difference.
Since my father was also the founder of the so-called [GERMAN], which was the sport club, and he belonged to the Veterans Association from World War I.
And there was no difference between Jews and Gentile before Hitler.
Yes.
In their homes, and they came in our house.
Prior to 1935, I belonged to the sport club, which was Gentile and Jews mixed.
Yes.
Mostly gymnastics on handbar.
Well, we work in the garden.
We learned everything what has to do with the garden, planting vegetables, and we were self-sufficient like a farmer because there was so much land.
In the harvest we were helping, and since my father was a cattle dealer, we needed a lot of hay, and so we harvested it and everything.
Very few because she died in 1925 of cancer, but my father married later on the sister of my mother, who was a widow.
Well, I was six years old, and she spent maybe a year in the hospital.
So I remember that we went to the hospital nearly every day after school.
That's all I can remember.
Yes.
Every holiday we went to the synagogue, and we were specially dressed in good clothing, and so were all the Jews in the town.
And we came together because it was just like one family.
The local newspaper, and we had [INAUDIBLE], Theodor Storm, and [INAUDIBLE], which was-- had written all the stories about American Indians.
Yes, and history.
In 1932 already in high school, we had already swastikas painted on our desks, and some teacher told my father they should take us out of school because we had no chance to go ahead.
Some teachers were already anti-Semitic, and we were getting poor marks.
There was what you call [GERMAN], similar like a gymnasium.
Gymnasium they were learning as the major language Latin, and we were learning French and English instead.
Very disciplined, and you had to pass a test before you even could get into high school.
And your parents had to pay for it.
And if you would flunk out in the third year in high school, you had to leave high school.
Yes.
I was out of high school.
My father took me in his business to learn the cattle business.
Yes, I did, for two years.
And I was the only Jewish boy in that class, and most of the time I was disregarded by the teacher.
Well, they were teaching [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] what they call in German Rassenkunde.
The Jews were second class citizens.
They were different and everything.
And some teachers were good.
They would not make any difference, or they would disregard you completely.
Yes.
[INAUDIBLE] School was only in the afternoons three days a week.
Yes.
It was discussed when everyone said I'll leave, they will not last long.
Because the government in Germany during the time when I can remember sometimes would not last six months or not longer than a year.
Then the government would fall.
And they said the same will happen to Hitler and his gang, also.
Of course, the Depression was on, a lot of unemployment, and a lot of people were poor.
A certain amount did, a certain amount did not.
Some in the public offices changed.
There were open feelings of anti-Semitism against the Jews.
You had-- when you went in, you had to wait to talk to them, or they would treat you like dirt.
Not till '35.
After that, you could not go in a restaurant anymore.
Say in a bar or something, you were insulted and things like that.
That started after '35 after the Nuremberg Laws went into effect.
Also, I had some sense I was going out with, a Gentile, and I got told not to go anymore because he had sisters, and it would be a question if I will go out with a girl or with a boy.
Some Jews welcomed the Nuremberg Laws because there was a lot of intermarriage going on in Germany, and they said it's good.
It will stop the intermarriages in Germany.
In the beginning, not.
Later on, yes.
We found out we were second class citizens.
We wanted to leave Germany.
But my father did not want to leave Germany because the business was still good, and he was a soldier in the First World War with seven of his brothers.
There were eight brothers who were soldiers.
Two were killed on the War, and he was 100% German first and Jew second.
Yes.
German.
But when we saw what was going on, we wanted to get out of Germany, and we started rebelling.
My twin brother was the first one to rebel, and he left Germany.
He joined the Foreign Legion, then became a French citizen afterwards because we had relatives in France who made the arrangement when he stayed in the French army.
And he got killed in May, 1945, fighting against the Japanese.
Well, my other brother, Bruno, he got a-- had an argument with an SS-- member of the SS-- and he got accused of so-called Rassenschande, and he had to leave Germany illegally in 1936.
And he had to go over the border by the night not to be arrested by the Gestapo.
And he went to Chile.
Santiago Chile.
Rassenschande, even though he had no date with a German girl.
He had nothing.
Yeah.
Some farmers who owed my father money would not pay up anymore.
Even if you would take them to court, the court was against the Jews.
I would say after '35 when the Nuremberg Laws went in effect.
Well, the first time we saw in 1933 when the [INAUDIBLE].
Then we had two members of the Nazi party standing in front of our house that nobody should go in and out.
That was in 1933 and April 1.
After that, we didn't hear or see anything till 1935.
Then we openly got insulted as dirty Jews and things like that.
Well, you could not go in a bar anymore later on and things like that, but otherwise, there were no prohibitions.
Not at that time.
Not in '35.

On a street nearby, and we didn't hear and see much of them, but when they marched with the-- through the streets and like that, the Jews made sure they were not on the streets and they were in the houses.
Yes, we heard rumors about concentration camps, and we know that some Gentiles were arrested, too.
Were communists and things like that, that they went on trial and went to jail, and things like that.
We heard about that, and also that some Jews got killed in the concentration camps.
And they all died of heart attacks or something else, they said, the Germans, but the bodies were not returned.
Only they could have the ashes of the bodies.
No.
Nothing till 1938.
In the pogrom of 1938.
Our house [INAUDIBLE] three rooms.
The furniture got smashed, the windows got smashed, and I got arrested.
So did my father.
And all other [INAUDIBLE] were arrested in town during that time.
My brother was-- oldest brother-- was in Westphalia.
He got arrested, and he got shipped out to Oranienburg concentration camp.
My father was sent to a prison nearby, and since I was still considered a minor they let me go.
In 1938, I was 20.
I was released.
And then I had to replace the windows and everything what was smashed.
So the Jews had to fix up the houses, and you could not get any outside help to do that.
My mother, my sister, and my youngest brother.
Yes, he remarried in 1927.
Fanny, and she was the sister of my first mother.
She was a widow.
My father was away close to three months, and they let him go under the condition that he would sell his property.
And the Nazi government set a certain price for the property, and the money went in a bank account which we could not touch.
The house, the barns, and some grazing land on-- on some farmland where we had a big garden, food garden where we were planting vegetables.
We were self-sufficient at that time.
Beginning of '39, and he had to sign everything over.
The Nazis bought it for 6,500 marks, which was confiscated.
I got it back later on when I sold it for over 40,000 marks.
We tried to get out of Germany, and we could not have any business anymore because all Jewish businesses were closed.
And I was forced to work for a farmer, and we would get minimum wage.
And then I had to-- a short time later, I had to work on the Autobahn, which was very difficult.
I had to go to Dusseldorf by train, and I had to work there six days a week.
By train.
Yes.
My oldest brother, he got a phony paper that he could go to South America.
He got released on the concentration camp, and he had to do forced labor in a swamp nearby.
The Germans were drying out a swamp.
And so was my youngest brother.
We could go home every day, but we had to do a certain amount of work every day for minimum wage.
And there was also a special tax for Jews because they were not-- not member of the Army, and there was an extra 15% tax taken off the salary for that purpose.
Yes.
We had no problem with that till the war started.
Then it was more difficult to get everything.
But since we were still self-sufficient with most of the things, we had no problems there.
But--
Yes, we remained in our own house.
We were disregarded and [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] in general, we in the town had no problem, but in the cities there were problems.
Jews were assaulted on the street and things like that.
No, but we got also a visa to go to Chile.
And we made the booking and everything, but the war broke out.
We could not get out of Germany anymore.
First it was in Dusseldorf.
Then I had to work for a while in the swamp Only Jews were working there.
And then later on, I had to work for a German company reclaiming and recycling.
And we were treated as second class citizen.
We had to do what they told us, and you could not open your mouth and answer back.

The first work detail was a contractor who was a member of the SS, and what we had to do, we had to do some road building in a swamp and had to take the rubble from the synagogues, which were destroyed in [INAUDIBLE], and we had to build road from that.
Let's say as long as you did not step out of line, you were treated like a human being.
We had rationing cards, and some stores had signs on it, Jews will not be served.
Other stores, we could get what we had on the ration card.
But in general, we had no shortage of food till 1941.
Then it was a different ballgame After we had to wear the Star of David.
How can we get out of Germany.
Yeah, but there was-- in the beginning, some people could get out.
The Germans let you get out till somewhere in 1941, people who could go to the United States, who had relatives who paid for the fare and things like that.
Some could go to Switzerland or Sweden, but very few because Switzerland, we know, was very anti-Semitic during that time.
Sweden, Norway, and Denmark were not anti-Semitic, and Holland was not either, but the Germans took Holland in 1940.
It was a different ballgame after that.
For nearly a year.
Then I worked for nearly a year in a swamp.
And then I worked for the reclaiming and recycling company for nearly a year.
Really, the change after Rosh Hashanah in 1941 when we have to wear the Star of David.
A cloth, yellow, with the Star of David printed in black, and then the German word for Jew, 'Jude,' was printed in, and you had to wear on the left-hand side of the vest.
On the outer clothing only.
Yes.
And we were restricted.
We had to be in the house at 8 o'clock in the evening.
You could not go out, and you could not leave the town unless you had a permit from the Gestapo.
Yes.
Yes.
The next action was that in December beginning-- no, end of November, 1941, we got notified we would be deported to Riga, Latvia, for to do work over there.
And at the same time, my parents got told, since they're over 60 years of age, if they want to they can stay back, but my parents decided to go with us.
It did not come in a form.
We were told to come to town hall to the police department there, and the fellow who was in charge there and also representing the Gestapo read us that we would be deported.
That we would be able to take 40 pounds of clothing along, plus blankets, and our food cards would be validated for six weeks so we could take additional food along.
We could take eating utensils along, and that was it.
No.
When we got the notice, my brother and I myself wanted to get married because we both were engaged.

My oldest brother got permission to get married because there was one official in the town hall where he was living was very friendly to the Jews despite the Nazis, and he got the Gestapo gave the permit for one to be married.
And they could get married within three days in Germany.
Otherwise, it takes two weeks to get the permit.
So they got married on December 6, 1941, and four days later, we got deported.
Yes.
The Gestapo denied it.
December 10, a policeman took us to [NON-ENGLISH], on a train to Dusseldorf, to the cattle house of the slaughterhouse.
We were received by the Gestapo and treated like cattle.
Everyone was frisk, and anything what the Germans thought was good for the war effort or things what they figured we didn't need was confiscated.
